title: How to Dual Boot Windows and Linux
description: Install Linux alongside Windows without losing your data. Step-by-step dual boot setup with GRUB.
prerequisites: A PC with Windows 10 or 11 installed
At least 50GB of free disk space

steps.title: Back Up Your Data
steps.content: Before touching partitions, back up anything important. Use an external drive, cloud storage, or both. Partitioning is generally safe but mistakes can happen.
steps.warning: Partitioning carries a small risk of data loss. Always back up first.

steps.title: Shrink Your Windows Partition
steps.content: Open **Disk Management** in Windows (right-click Start > Disk Management). Right-click your main partition (usually C:) and select **Shrink Volume**. Free up at least 50GB for Linux.

steps.title: Download a Linux ISO and Create a Bootable USB
steps.content: Download your preferred distro (Ubuntu, Mint, Fedora, etc.) and flash it to a USB drive using **balenaEtcher** or **Rufus** on Windows.
steps.code:
# On Linux, you can also use dd:
sudo dd if=ubuntu-24.04.iso of=/dev/sdX bs=4M status=progress
sync
steps.warning: Make sure you select the correct USB drive with dd — writing to the wrong device will erase it.

steps.title: Boot from USB
steps.content: Restart your PC and enter the boot menu (usually **F12**, **F2**, or **Del** during startup). Select your USB drive. If you have Secure Boot enabled and the installer doesn't load, you may need to disable it in BIOS.

steps.title: Install Linux Alongside Windows
steps.content:
In the installer, choose **Install alongside Windows** (or "Something else" for manual partitioning). The installer will detect Windows and set up the partition automatically.

For manual partitioning, create:
- **/** (root) — at least 30GB, ext4
- **swap** — equal to your RAM (or skip with a swap file)
- **/home** — remaining space, ext4 (optional but recommended)

steps.title: Configure GRUB Bootloader
steps.content: After installation, **GRUB** will be your bootloader. On every startup, you'll see a menu to choose between Linux and Windows. If Windows doesn't appear in the menu, run:

steps.title: Verify Both Systems Work
steps.content: Reboot and test both operating systems from the GRUB menu. Make sure Windows still boots correctly and your files are intact. In Linux, you can access your Windows files by mounting the NTFS partition.
steps.code:
# See your partitions:
lsblk
# Mount Windows partition (usually already auto-mounted):
sudo mount /dev/sda2 /mnt/windows

seo.title: How to Dual Boot Windows and Linux | Step-by-Step Guide
seo.description: Learn how to install Linux alongside Windows with dual boot. Complete guide with partitioning, USB creation, and GRUB setup.
